chunk_summary: This section discusses bedside nutrition assessment in chronic cardiorespiratory disease, focusing on identifying limiting factors through history, physical exam, and anthropometry to guide tailored nutritional interventions.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of triaging children with chronic cardiorespiratory disease for nutritional care.
Identify red flags such as feeding fatigue, malabsorption, and edema in nutrition assessment.
Learn to use anthropometric measures like MUAC, weight-for-length, and BMI for risk stratification.
Recognize the role of history-taking in diagnosing feeding difficulties and malnutrition.
key_facts: Feeding fatigue in tachypneic infants is indicated by feed durations >20–30 minutes.
Bulky, oily, foul stools may suggest cystic fibrosis (CF).
Mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) is used for children aged 6–59 months.
In CF, weight-for-length (WFL) should be ≥50th percentile for children under 2 years, and BMI ≥50th percentile for ages 2–20 years.
Edema may indicate malnutrition or cardiac failure and requires careful evaluation.
Diuretics can cause electrolyte depletion, and acid suppression should be used only when indicated.
reference_quote: High-quality nutrition care starts with triage that separates children who need monitored initiation from those who need rapid catch-up, while simultaneously identifying those in whom malabsorption or feeding safety dominates.
document_key_topics: Bedside nutrition assessment in chronic cardiorespiratory disease
Anthropometric measures for nutritional risk stratification
Role of history and physical exam in diagnosing malnutrition
